note_data.title: Other payables and other receivables
keyPoints: **Accruals** are expenses incurred during the accounting period but not yet paid, recorded as current liabilities
**Prepayments** are expenses paid in advance for future periods, recorded as current assets
**Other receivables** are amounts owed TO the business that are not trade debtors, such as rent receivable
**Other payables** are amounts owed BY the business that are not trade creditors, such as wages payable
Accruals increase expenses and increase liabilities in the statement of financial position
Prepayments reduce current period expenses and create assets in the statement of financial position
Year-end adjustments ensure expenses are matched to the correct accounting period following the **matching principle**
Insurance, rent, and telephone are common examples requiring accrual or prepayment adjustments
The **going concern concept** assumes the business will continue, justifying prepayment treatment as assets

definitions.definition: An expense that has been incurred during the accounting period but has not yet been paid
A payment made in advance for goods or services to be received in a future accounting period
Amounts owed to the business that are not trade debtors, such as rent receivable or insurance claims
Amounts owed by the business that are not trade creditors, such as accrued expenses or wages payable
The accounting concept that expenses should be matched against the revenues of the same accounting period

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Show workings clearly. State the expense for the year and accrual/prepayment amounts
Show the journal entry or ledger account entries with clear debit and credit entries

modelAnswer.question: Ahmed's business pays rent quarterly in advance. On 1 January 2023, rent of $3,600 for three months was paid. Calculate the prepayment at 31 January 2023 and explain how it should be treated in the financial statements.
modelAnswer.answer: Prepayment calculation: Monthly rent = $3,600 ÷ 3 = $1,200. Two months remain unpaid (February and March) = 2 × $1,200 = $2,400. The prepayment of $2,400 should be shown as a current asset in the statement of financial position because it represents rent paid in advance for future periods. The rent expense for January would be $1,200 in the statement of profit or loss.
markBreakdown: Mark 4: Explanation that it represents future periods

commonMistakes: Never say accruals are assets — they are always liabilities representing amounts owed
Students often confuse accruals with prepayments — accruals are unpaid expenses, prepayments are paid in advance
Never include accruals and prepayments with trade debtors/creditors — they are separate items
Students forget to adjust the expense account — both the balance sheet AND income statement are affected
Never ignore timing — always check which accounting period the expense relates to
examinerTips: This topic appears frequently in Paper 2, often worth 4-6 marks
Always show your workings when calculating monthly/daily amounts
Draw T-accounts to show the double entry effect clearly
Remember accruals increase both expenses and liabilities
formulas: Accrual = Unpaid expense for current period
Prepayment = Payment made for future periods
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: T-account showing expense account with cash payments, accruals carried down as credit balance (liability), and prepayments carried down as debit balance (asset). Statement of financial position extract showing accruals under current liabilities and prepayments under current assets.
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q3 (4 marks) — they asked students to prepare a statement of profit or loss extract including rent receivable with accruals and prepayments adjustments
N 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — multiple choice question testing understanding of correct statements about accruals and prepayments definitions
M/J 2024 P2 Q2 (6 marks) — candidates had to identify and adjust for insurance prepaid and electricity accrued in trial balance corrections
N 2023 P2 Q4 (8 marks) — comprehensive question requiring adjustments for rent receivable owing, advertising prepaid, and wages accrued in financial statements
M/J 2022 P1 Q18 (1 mark) — tested classification of prepaid expenses and accrued income in statement of financial position

workedExample.question: Ahmed's trial balance shows Insurance $2,400 (debit) and Rent receivable $1,800 (credit). Additional information: Insurance prepaid $300; Rent receivable outstanding $450. Prepare extracts showing these items in the statement of profit or loss and statement of financial position. (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: Calculate adjusted insurance expense: $2,400 - $300 prepaid = $2,100 expense for the year
Step 2: Calculate adjusted rent receivable income: $1,800 + $450 outstanding = $2,250 income for the year
Step 3: Show insurance expense $2,100 in statement of profit or loss (expenses section)
Step 4: Show rent receivable income $2,250 in statement of profit or loss (income section)
Step 5: Show prepaid insurance $300 as current asset in statement of financial position
Step 6: Show rent receivable outstanding $450 as current asset in statement of financial position
workedExample.answer: Statement of profit or loss extract: Income - Rent receivable $2,250; Expenses - Insurance $2,100. Statement of financial position extract: Current assets - Prepaid insurance $300, Rent receivable $450
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark each for: correct insurance expense calculation, correct rent receivable calculation, proper classification in profit/loss statement, proper classification as current assets

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused accrued expenses with prepaid expenses, showing them on wrong sides of accounts' — always remember accruals are amounts OWING (liabilities) while prepayments are amounts PAID IN ADVANCE (assets)

Examiner Report 2024: 'Students frequently failed to distinguish between accrued income and prepaid income' — accrued income is income EARNED but not yet received (asset), prepaid income is income RECEIVED but not yet earned (liability)

Examiner Report 2022: 'Common error was placing prepayments and accruals in wrong sections of statement of financial position' — always classify prepaid expenses and accrued income as CURRENT ASSETS, accrued expenses and prepaid income as CURRENT LIABILITIES
note_data.keyDiagram: T-account diagram showing four scenarios: 1) Prepaid Expense (Asset side: 'Prepaid Insurance $X') 2) Accrued Expense (Liability side: 'Wages Owing $X') 3) Accrued Income (Asset side: 'Rent Receivable $X') 4) Prepaid Income (Liability side: 'Rent Received in Advance $X'). Each T-account should show the adjustment process from trial balance figure to final statement figures, with arrows indicating whether amounts are added or subtracted.
note_data.quickRecap: Other payables are accrued expenses (amounts owed but not yet paid) and prepaid income (amounts received but not yet earned) - both are current liabilities. Other receivables are accrued income (amounts earned but not yet received) and prepaid expenses (amounts paid but not yet used) - both are current assets. Always adjust trial balance figures before preparing final statements. Remember: if it's owed TO you or paid BY you in advance = asset; if it's owed BY you or paid TO you in advance = liability.
